topic: European digital speech & political content moderation – last 7 days

articles.headline_en: Reform UK candidate condemned for ‘appalling’ social media posts, many now deleted
articles.summary_en: The Independent reports that Reform UK’s candidate for the Makerfield by‑election, Robert Kenyon, has been criticised for historic social media activity described as containing transphobic slurs, Covid‑19 misinformation and sexist content.[3] Many of the posts highlighted by opponents and campaigners have since been deleted from his accounts.[3] Party representatives have faced questions over vetting and whether the material is compatible with holding public office.[3]
articles.significance: The case illustrates how deleted or historic social media posts remain central to evaluating political candidates and can quickly become a reputational and electoral issue even after removal.

editorial_overview: Searches over the past week produced only one clearly relevant, recent item from an established European outlet directly matching the user’s criteria: a UK by‑election candidate’s deleted and controversial social media posts. Within the limits of available reporting, most current coverage focuses on political accountability for past online statements rather than on formal government-ordered content removal or court-driven takedown of political speech. Broader discussions of platform moderation and digital appeals in Europe exist but, in the last week, they have not been tightly tied to specific fresh incidents involving particular politicians or rulings that met the requested criteria. Overall, the week’s publicly visible European news on this theme is sparse and largely concentrated on domestic political vetting rather than systemic censorship or landmark moderation disputes.
